lesson.title: The OSI Model
lesson.objective: Name the 7 layers of the OSI model in order
Describe the primary function of each OSI layer
Explain how data encapsulation works through the OSI layers
lesson.note: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a conceptual framework that standardizes how different network systems communicate. It was developed by the ISO in 1984.

diagram.caption: The 7 layers of the OSI Reference Model

lesson.diagram: Layer 7 — Application (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS) Layer 6 — Presentation (TLS/SSL, JPEG, ASCII) Layer 5 — Session (NetBIOS, RPC) Layer 4 — Transport (TCP, UDP) Layer 3 — Network (IP, ICMP, routing) Layer 2 — Data Link (Ethernet, MAC, ARP) Layer 1 — Physical (Cables, hubs, bits)
lesson.definition: The process by which each OSI layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data received from the layer above, creating a Protocol Data Unit (PDU).
lesson.example: When you send an email: - Layer 7 (Application): Creates the email message (SMTP) - Layer 4 (Transport): Breaks it into segments, adds port numbers - Layer 3 (Network): Adds IP source and destination addresses - Layer 2 (Data Link): Adds MAC addresses for local delivery - Layer 1 (Physical): Converts to electrical signals on the wire
lesson.note: Use the mnemonic "All People Seem To Need Data Processing" (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical) to remember the layers top-down.
The OSI model is a reference model. In practice, the TCP/IP model (4 layers) is what the internet actually uses. The OSI model is still essential for understanding and troubleshooting.

question.text: Which OSI layer is responsible for IP addressing and routing?
TCP and UDP operate at which OSI layer?
What is the PDU name at the Transport layer?

lesson.exercise: For each of the following protocols, identify which OSI layer it operates at: HTTP, IP, Ethernet, TCP, TLS, DNS. Create a table mapping each protocol to its layer number and name.

summary.item: The OSI model has 7 layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Each layer has specific responsibilities and communicates with adjacent layers
Data encapsulation adds headers at each layer going down; headers are removed going up
Mnemonic (bottom-up): Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away
